findings.title: Potential SQL Injection (CWE-89)
findings.description:
**Potential SQL Injection Vulnerability**

**Pattern detected**: Go fmt.Sprintf with string interpolation in SQL query

**Location**: pkg/gui/controllers/helpers/branches_helper.go:291

**Code snippet**:
```go
updateCommands += fmt.Sprintf("update %s %s %s\n", branch.FullRefName(), branch.FullUpstreamRefName(), branch.CommitHash)
```

SQL injection occurs when untrusted input is incorporated into SQL queries without
proper sanitization. An attacker could manipulate the query to:
- Access unauthorized data
- Modify or delete database records
- Execute administrative operations
- In some cases, execute operating system commands

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special
Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')**.
findings.suggested_fix:
**Recommended fixes**:

1. **Use parameterized queries** (preferred):
```go
// Instead of:
query := fmt.Sprintf("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s", id)
db.Query(query)

// Use:
db.Query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1", id)
```

2. **Use prepared statements**:
```go
stmt, err := db.Prepare("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?")
rows, err := stmt.Query(id)
```

3. **Use sqlx named parameters**:
```go
query := "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = :id"
rows, err := db.NamedQuery(query, map[string]interface{}{"id": id})
```

4. **Validate and sanitize input** when parameterization is not possible.
findings.why_it_matters: SQL injection is one of the most dangerous vulnerabilities, allowing attackers to access, modify, or delete sensitive data in the database.

findings.title: Potential ReDoS vulnerability
findings.description:
Regex with nested quantifiers may cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to denial of service.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern issue: nested quantifiers detected
📝 Pattern: ``
🎯 User input detected in context
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite regex to avoid nested quantifiers and add input length limits.
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 3 functions in call cycle (complexity 34)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 3 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/lazygit/pkg/app/app.go::(*App).setupRepo:182 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/lazygit/pkg/app/app.go::NewApp:95 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/lazygit/pkg/app/app.go::Run:40. Aggregate complexity: 34.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.
